Define Signatures for Service Stored Procedures

Create a signature for a service stored procedure, in which one or more stored procedures are represented to Uniface as operations.

Develop and thoroughly test the DBMS stored procedure(s). For information on DBMS-specific implementation and operation requirements, see the relevant DBMS connector information. For more information, see SQL and Stored Procedures Support.

Signatures make it possible to treat stored procedures as any other Uniface component and invoke them using the activate ProcScript instruction. When defining the component, operations, and parameters, take into account the Uniface names are always upper case. This can affect the actual names used when activating service stored procedures. For more information, see Naming Specifications for Service Store Procedures.

To define a signature for stored procedures:

  1. Create a Stored Procedure Component.
    1. Open the Signature Editor and choose File >New
    2. Enter a component Name.

      The component name can be anything, but you may want to use the name of the package or schema (or library, on iSeries) in which the stored procedure resides.

    3. In the Type field, select Stored Procedure Component. Then click OK.
  2. Optionally, map the Uniface Component Name to a package, schema, or library.
    1. Click >> adjacent to the Implementations field and click Properties.
    2. Enter the actual package or schema name in Literal Component Name and click OK.
      If you leave this field empty, Uniface uses the default schema.
    3. Click OK to return to the main Signature Editor
  3. Define an operation for each stored procedure.
    1. Click >> adjacent to the Details of Operation field.
    2. Click New and set the following provide an Operation Name for each stored procedure.
    3. Optionally, set the Communication Default and specify a Literal Name for each operation.
      The Literal Name is the name of the stored procedure as it is known by the database. If you specify the literal operation name, the connector assumes that you want explicit control over the exact stored procedure identifier. This can be either SchemaName.OperationName, or just OperationName, in which case the default schema is used.
    4. Click OK.
  4. For each operation, define the required parameters.
    The operation parameters must match the parameters of the stored procedure in number (that is, there must be exactly as many parameters defined in the operation as the stored procedure has), direction (that is, IN, OUTor INOUTparameter) and data type.
    1. Click >> adjacent to the Parameter fields and click New.
    2. For each parameter, provide a Parameter Name, and select the Data Type, and Direction.
      For example, a DB2 stored procedure with an input parameter of SQL type Varchar(40) must be represented in the signature as an in parameter with a data type String. An output parameter of SQL type DECIMAL(3.2) must be represented as an out parameter with data type Numeric.
  5. Compile your stored procedure signature.
Related Links
  • Define a Component Signature

    Use the Signature Editor to create or modify component signatures. Signatures of Uniface components are automatically generated during compilation. Signatures of non-Uniface components can be created or imported.
